title: Neural Network Tuning of FSMPC for Drives
summary: This preprint presents a neural network tuner for the finite state model predictive control of an induction motor. The tuner deals with the parameters of the controllers in the speed loop and in the stator current loop. The results are assessed using a five phase machine in an experimental setup. Data for the neural network training is obtained from the experiments using step tests.

AI.tldr: Neural network tuner for finite state model predictive control of induction motors, handling speed and stator current loop parameters, validated on five-phase machine experimental setup.
AI.motivation: To develop an automated tuning method for finite state model predictive control (FS-MPC) of induction motors, addressing the challenge of manually tuning controller parameters for optimal performance in both speed and stator current loops.
AI.method: A neural network-based tuner is designed to adjust FS-MPC parameters. Training data is collected from experimental step tests on a five-phase induction motor setup, allowing the neural network to learn optimal parameter mappings from system responses.
AI.result: The neural network tuner is successfully implemented and evaluated on an experimental five-phase induction motor setup, demonstrating effective parameter tuning for the FS-MPC controllers in both speed and current control loops.
AI.conclusion: Neural network-based tuning provides an effective automated approach for optimizing finite state model predictive control parameters in induction motor drives, validated through experimental testing on a five-phase machine.

title: Differentiable Stochastic Traffic Dynamics: Physics-Informed Generative Modelling in Transportation
summary: Macroscopic traffic flow is stochastic, but the physics-informed deep learning methods currently used in transportation literature embed deterministic PDEs and produce point-valued outputs; the stochasticity of the governing dynamics plays no role in the learned representation. This work develops a framework in which the physics constraint itself is distributional and directly derived from stochastic traffic-flow dynamics. Starting from an Ito-type Lighthill-Whitham-Richards model with Brownian forcing, we derive a one-point forward equation for the marginal traffic density at each spatial location. The spatial coupling induced by the conservation law appears as an explicit conditional drift term, which makes the closure requirement transparent. Based on this formulation, we derive an equivalent deterministic Probability Flow ODE that is pointwise evaluable and differentiable once a closure is specified. Incorporating this as a physics constraint, we then propose a score network with an advection-closure module, trainable by denoising score matching together with a Fokker-Planck residual loss. The resulting model targets a data-conditioned density distribution, from which point estimates, credible intervals, and congestion-risk measures can be computed. The framework provides a basis for distributional traffic-state estimation and for stochastic fundamental-diagram analysis in a physics-informed generative setting.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed generative framework for distributional traffic-state estimation using stochastic PDEs and score networks, enabling uncertainty quantification in traffic flow modeling.
AI.motivation: Current physics-informed deep learning methods for traffic flow use deterministic PDEs and produce point-valued outputs, ignoring the inherent stochasticity of traffic dynamics. There's a need to incorporate stochasticity directly into the physics constraints for proper uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: Derived a one-point forward equation from an Ito-type Lighthill-Whitham-Richards model with Brownian forcing, then developed an equivalent deterministic Probability Flow ODE. Incorporated this as physics constraint in a score network with advection-closure module, trainable via denoising score matching and Fokker-Planck residual loss.
AI.result: The framework produces data-conditioned density distributions from which point estimates, credible intervals, and congestion-risk measures can be computed, enabling distributional traffic-state estimation and stochastic fundamental-diagram analysis.
AI.conclusion: The proposed physics-informed generative framework successfully incorporates stochastic traffic-flow dynamics into deep learning models, providing a foundation for uncertainty-aware traffic analysis and stochastic fundamental diagram studies.

title: Optimization-Based Formation Flight on Libration Point Orbits
summary: A model predictive control (MPC) framework is developed for station-keeping in spacecraft formation flight along libration point orbits. At each control period, the MPC policy solves a multi-vehicle optimal control problem (MVOCP) that tracks a reference trajectory, while enforcing path constraints on the relative motion of the formation. The control policy makes use of a limited set of control nodes consistent with operational constraints that allow only a small number of maneuver opportunities per revolution. To promote recursive feasibility, path constraints are progressively tightened across the prediction horizon. An isoperimetric reformulation of the constraints is used to prevent inter-sample violations. The resulting MVOCP is a nonconvex program, which is solved via sequential convex programming. The proposed approach is evaluated in a high-fidelity ephemeris model under realistic uncertainties for a formation along the near-rectilinear halo orbit (NRHO), and subject to path constraints on interspacecraft separation and relative Sun phase angle. The results demonstrate maintenance of a spacecraft formation that satisfies the path constraints with cumulative propellant consumption comparable to that of existing methods

AI.tldr: MPC framework for spacecraft formation station-keeping along libration point orbits using sequential convex programming to solve nonconvex optimal control problems with path constraints.
AI.motivation: To develop a control framework for maintaining spacecraft formations along libration point orbits (specifically NRHO) that can handle operational constraints like limited maneuver opportunities while enforcing path constraints on relative motion and Sun phase angles.
AI.method: Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework solving multi-vehicle optimal control problems (MVOCP) with limited control nodes, progressive constraint tightening for recursive feasibility, isoperimetric constraint reformulation to prevent inter-sample violations, and sequential convex programming to solve the nonconvex optimization.
AI.result: The approach successfully maintains spacecraft formations satisfying path constraints on interspacecraft separation and relative Sun phase angle in high-fidelity ephemeris models with realistic uncertainties, with propellant consumption comparable to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MPC framework provides an effective solution for station-keeping in spacecraft formations along libration point orbits, capable of handling operational constraints and path constraints while maintaining reasonable propellant efficiency.

title: Universal Speech Content Factorization
summary: We propose Universal Speech Content Factorization (USCF), a simple and invertible linear method for extracting a low-rank speech representation in which speaker timbre is suppressed while phonetic content is preserved. USCF extends Speech Content Factorization, a closed-set voice conversion (VC) method, to an open-set setting by learning a universal speech-to-content mapping via least-squares optimization and deriving speaker-specific transformations from only a few seconds of target speech. We show through embedding analysis that USCF effectively removes speaker-dependent variation. As a zero-shot VC system, USCF achieves competitive intelligibility, naturalness, and speaker similarity compared to methods that require substantially more target-speaker data or additional neural training. Finally, we demonstrate that as a training-efficient timbre-disentangled speech feature, USCF features can serve as the acoustic representation for training timbre-prompted text-to-speech models. Speech samples and code are publicly available.

AI.tldr: USCF is a linear, invertible method for extracting low-rank speech representations that suppress speaker timbre while preserving phonetic content, enabling zero-shot voice conversion and timbre-disentangled speech features.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a universal method for extracting speaker-independent phonetic content from speech, extending previous closed-set voice conversion approaches to open-set scenarios while requiring minimal target-speaker data.
AI.method: USCF learns a universal speech-to-content mapping via least-squares optimization, deriving speaker-specific transformations from only a few seconds of target speech. It extends Speech Content Factorization to open-set settings through a linear, invertible approach.
AI.result: USCF effectively removes speaker-dependent variation (verified through embedding analysis), achieves competitive intelligibility, naturalness, and speaker similarity in zero-shot VC compared to methods requiring more data or neural training, and serves as effective timbre-disentangled features for TTS models.
AI.conclusion: USCF provides a simple, effective solution for universal speech content factorization that enables zero-shot voice conversion with minimal data requirements and serves as a useful timbre-disentangled representation for speech synthesis applications.

title: EDMFormer: Genre-Specific Self-Supervised Learning for Music Structure Segmentation
summary: Music structure segmentation is a key task in audio analysis, but existing models perform poorly on Electronic Dance Music (EDM). This problem exists because most approaches rely on lyrical or harmonic similarity, which works well for pop music but not for EDM. EDM structure is instead defined by changes in energy, rhythm, and timbre, with different sections such as buildup, drop, and breakdown. We introduce EDMFormer, a transformer model that combines self-supervised audio embeddings using an EDM-specific dataset and taxonomy. We release this dataset as EDM-98: a group of 98 professionally annotated EDM tracks. EDMFormer improves boundary detection and section labelling compared to existing models, particularly for drops and buildups. The results suggest that combining learned representations with genre-specific data and structural priors is effective for EDM and could be applied to other specialized music genres or broader audio domains.

AI.tldr: EDMFormer: A transformer model using self-supervised audio embeddings and EDM-specific dataset (EDM-98) to improve music structure segmentation for Electronic Dance Music, which traditional models handle poorly.
AI.motivation: Existing music structure segmentation models perform poorly on Electronic Dance Music because they rely on lyrical or harmonic similarity, which doesn't capture EDM's structure defined by energy, rhythm, and timbre changes (buildup, drop, breakdown sections).
AI.method: EDMFormer combines self-supervised audio embeddings with an EDM-specific dataset (EDM-98: 98 professionally annotated EDM tracks) and taxonomy using transformer architecture to better detect boundaries and label sections.
AI.result: EDMFormer improves boundary detection and section labeling compared to existing models, particularly for drops and buildups, demonstrating better performance on EDM structure segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Combining learned representations with genre-specific data and structural priors is effective for EDM segmentation and could be applied to other specialized music genres or broader audio domains.

title: Safe or Slow? The Illusion of Thermal Stability Under Reduced-Velocity Nail Intrusion
summary: This study investigates the effects of nail penetration speed on the safety outcomes of large-format automotive lithium-ion pouch cells. Through six controlled tests varying the speed of nail insertion, we observed that lower penetration speeds did not induce thermal runaway; instead, the cells exhibited self-discharge while the nail remained embedded. These findings suggest that penetration speed is a critical factor in the onset of thermal runaway, providing valuable insights for the development of safer battery systems and more effective safety testing protocols.

AI.tldr: Lower nail penetration speeds prevent thermal runaway in large-format lithium-ion pouch cells, causing only self-discharge instead.
AI.motivation: To understand how nail penetration speed affects safety outcomes in large-format automotive lithium-ion pouch cells, particularly regarding thermal runaway initiation.
AI.method: Conducted six controlled tests varying nail insertion speed on large-format automotive lithium-ion pouch cells.
AI.result: Lower penetration speeds did not induce thermal runaway; cells exhibited self-discharge while the nail remained embedded.
AI.conclusion: Penetration speed is a critical factor in thermal runaway onset, providing insights for safer battery systems and improved safety testing protocols.

title: Trade-offs Between Capacity and Robustness in Neural Audio Codecs for Adversarially Robust Speech Recognition
summary: Adversarial perturbations exploit vulnerabilities in automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems while preserving human perceived linguistic content. Neural audio codecs impose a discrete bottleneck that can suppress fine-grained signal variations associated with adversarial noise. We examine how the granularity of this bottleneck, controlled by residual vector quantization (RVQ) depth, shapes adversarial robustness. We observe a non-monotonic trade-off under gradient-based attacks: shallow quantization suppresses adversarial perturbations but degrades speech content, while deeper quantization preserves both content and perturbations. Intermediate depths balance these effects and minimize transcription error. We further show that adversarially induced changes in discrete codebook tokens strongly correlate with transcription error. These gains persist under adaptive attacks, where neural codec configurations outperform traditional compression defenses.

AI.tldr: Neural audio codecs with intermediate residual vector quantization depths provide optimal adversarial robustness for ASR systems by balancing speech quality preservation and perturbation suppression.
AI.motivation: Adversarial perturbations exploit ASR system vulnerabilities while preserving human-perceived content. Neural audio codecs with discrete bottlenecks may suppress adversarial noise, but the relationship between quantization granularity and robustness needs investigation.
AI.method: Examine how residual vector quantization (RVQ) depth controls bottleneck granularity in neural audio codecs. Analyze trade-offs under gradient-based attacks: shallow vs. deep quantization effects on adversarial perturbations and speech content preservation. Investigate correlation between adversarial changes in discrete codebook tokens and transcription error.
AI.result: Non-monotonic trade-off observed: shallow quantization suppresses perturbations but degrades speech content; deep quantization preserves both content and perturbations. Intermediate RVQ depths balance these effects and minimize transcription error. Adversarial changes in discrete tokens strongly correlate with transcription error. Neural codec configurations outperform traditional compression defenses under adaptive attacks.
AI.conclusion: Neural audio codecs with intermediate quantization depths provide effective defense against adversarial attacks on ASR systems by optimally balancing content preservation and perturbation suppression, outperforming traditional compression-based defenses.

title: Fish Audio S2 Technical Report
summary: We introduce Fish Audio S2, an open-sourced text-to-speech system featuring multi-speaker, multi-turn generation, and, most importantly, instruction-following control via natural-language descriptions. To scale training, we develop a multi-stage training recipe together with a staged data pipeline covering video captioning and speech captioning, voice-quality assessment, and reward modeling. To push the frontier of open-source TTS, we release our model weights, fine-tuning code, and an SGLang-based inference engine. The inference engine is production-ready for streaming, achieving an RTF of 0.195 and a time-to-first-audio below 100 ms.Our code and weights are available on GitHub (https://github.com/fishaudio/fish-speech) and Hugging Face (https://huggingface.co/fishaudio/s2-pro). We highly encourage readers to visit https://fish.audio to try custom voices.

AI.tldr: Fish Audio S2 is an open-source text-to-speech system with multi-speaker, multi-turn generation and natural language instruction-following control, featuring production-ready inference with low latency.
AI.motivation: To advance open-source TTS capabilities by creating a system that can handle complex scenarios like multi-speaker conversations and respond to natural language instructions, while maintaining production-ready performance.
AI.method: Multi-stage training recipe with staged data pipeline covering video captioning, speech captioning, voice-quality assessment, and reward modeling. Uses SGLang-based inference engine optimized for streaming.
AI.result: Achieves RTF of 0.195 and time-to-first-audio below 100 ms. System supports multi-speaker, multi-turn generation with instruction-following control via natural language descriptions.
AI.conclusion: Fish Audio S2 pushes the frontier of open-source TTS by providing production-ready, high-performance speech synthesis with advanced control capabilities, with all code and weights publicly released.

title: Amplitude Dependent Bode Diagrams via Scaled Relative Graphs
comment: Submitted for Conference on Decision and Control 2026
summary: Scaled Relative Graphs (SRGs) provide an intuitive graphical frequency-domain method for the analysis of Nonlinear (NL) systems, generalizing the Nyquist diagram. In this paper, we develop a method for computing $L_2$-gain bounds for Lur'e systems over bounded frequency and amplitude ranges. We do this by restricting the input space of the SRG both in frequency and energy content, and combining with methods from Sobolev theory. The resulting gain bounds over restricted sets of inputs are less conservative than bounds computed over all of $L_2$, and yield three-dimensional NL generalization of the Bode diagram, plotting $L_2$-gain as function of both input frequency and energy content. In the zero-energy limit, the Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Bode diagram is recovered, and at the infinite-energy zero-frequency limit, we recover the $L_2$-gain. The effectiveness of our method is demonstrated on an example that resembles Phase-Locked Loop dynamics.

AI.tldr: SRG-based method computes L₂-gain bounds for Lur'e systems over bounded frequency/amplitude ranges, yielding 3D nonlinear Bode diagrams less conservative than full L₂ bounds.
AI.motivation: Existing L₂-gain bounds for nonlinear systems computed over all L₂ inputs can be overly conservative; need less conservative bounds over restricted input spaces (frequency and amplitude ranges) for practical applications.
AI.method: Combine Scaled Relative Graphs (SRGs) with Sobolev theory, restricting input space in both frequency and energy content to compute L₂-gain bounds for Lur'e systems over bounded frequency/amplitude ranges.
AI.result: Method yields 3D nonlinear generalization of Bode diagram plotting L₂-gain as function of input frequency and energy; recovers LTI Bode diagram at zero-energy limit and L₂-gain at infinite-energy zero-frequency limit; demonstrated effective on Phase-Locked Loop-like example.
AI.conclusion: SRG-based approach with restricted input spaces provides less conservative gain bounds than full L₂ analysis, enabling practical nonlinear frequency-domain analysis with connections to classical linear theory.

title: Emotion-Aware Prefix: Towards Explicit Emotion Control in Voice Conversion Models
summary: Recent advances in zero-shot voice conversion have exhibited potential in emotion control, yet the performance is suboptimal or inconsistent due to their limited expressive capacity. We propose Emotion-Aware Prefix for explicit emotion control in a two-stage voice conversion backbone. We significantly improve emotion conversion performance, doubling the baseline Emotion Conversion Accuracy (ECA) from 42.40% to 85.50% while maintaining linguistic integrity and speech quality, without compromising speaker identity. Our ablation study suggests that a joint control of both sequence modulation and acoustic realization is essential to synthesize distinct emotions. Furthermore, comparative analysis verifies the generalizability of proposed method, while it provides insights on the role of acoustic decoupling in maintaining speaker identity.

AI.tldr: Emotion-Aware Prefix improves zero-shot voice conversion by explicitly controlling emotion through sequence modulation and acoustic realization, doubling emotion conversion accuracy while preserving linguistic integrity and speaker identity.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot voice conversion methods have limited expressive capacity for emotion control, resulting in suboptimal or inconsistent performance. There's a need for better explicit emotion control in voice conversion systems.
AI.method: Proposes Emotion-Aware Prefix for explicit emotion control in a two-stage voice conversion backbone. The method employs joint control of both sequence modulation and acoustic realization to synthesize distinct emotions while maintaining speaker identity.
AI.result: Significantly improves emotion conversion performance, doubling the baseline Emotion Conversion Accuracy (ECA) from 42.40% to 85.50%. Maintains linguistic integrity and speech quality without compromising speaker identity. Ablation study shows joint control of sequence modulation and acoustic realization is essential.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Emotion-Aware Prefix effectively enables explicit emotion control in voice conversion, with strong generalizability. Provides insights on acoustic decoupling's role in maintaining speaker identity while achieving high emotion conversion accuracy.

title: VoxEmo: Benchmarking Speech Emotion Recognition with Speech LLMs
summary: Speech Large Language Models (LLMs) show great promise for speech emotion recognition (SER) via generative interfaces. However, shifting from closed-set classification to open text generation introduces zero-shot stochasticity, making evaluation highly sensitive to prompts. Additionally, conventional speech LLMs benchmarks overlook the inherent ambiguity of human emotion. Hence, we present VoxEmo, a comprehensive SER benchmark encompassing 35 emotion corpora across 15 languages for Speech LLMs. VoxEmo provides a standardized toolkit featuring varying prompt complexities, from direct classification to paralinguistic reasoning. To reflect real-world perception/application, we introduce a distribution-aware soft-label protocol and a prompt-ensemble strategy that emulates annotator disagreement. Experiments reveal that while zero-shot speech LLMs trail supervised baselines in hard-label accuracy, they uniquely align with human subjective distributions.

AI.tldr: VoxEmo is a comprehensive speech emotion recognition benchmark for speech LLMs, addressing zero-shot stochasticity and emotion ambiguity through standardized prompts, distribution-aware soft-label evaluation, and prompt-ensemble strategies.
AI.motivation: Speech LLMs show promise for SER via generative interfaces, but shifting from closed-set classification to open text generation introduces zero-shot stochasticity and prompt sensitivity. Existing benchmarks overlook the inherent ambiguity of human emotion, necessitating a more comprehensive evaluation framework.
AI.method: VoxEmo benchmark with 35 emotion corpora across 15 languages, featuring standardized toolkit with varying prompt complexities (direct classification to paralinguistic reasoning). Introduces distribution-aware soft-label protocol and prompt-ensemble strategy to emulate annotator disagreement.
AI.result: Zero-shot speech LLMs trail supervised baselines in hard-label accuracy but uniquely align with human subjective distributions, demonstrating their ability to capture emotion ambiguity better than traditional classification approaches.
AI.conclusion: VoxEmo provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating speech LLMs on emotion recognition, addressing key challenges of prompt sensitivity and emotion ambiguity while revealing that speech LLMs better capture human subjective emotion distributions despite lower hard-label accuracy.

title: Existence and Design of Functional Observers for Time-Delay Systems with Delayed Output Measurements
summary:
This paper investigates the problem of functional state estimation for linear time-delay systems in which the delay affecting the state evolution differs from the delay affecting the output measurements. While existing observer designs typically assume instantaneous output availability, practical systems often exhibit measurement delays that are distinct from and not aligned with the intrinsic state delay. We explicitly distinguish between the state delay $τ$ and the measurement delay $h$ and address the problem of estimating a desired functional $z(t)=Fx(t)$ under such mismatched delay conditions.
  Three functional observer structures are proposed to accommodate different delay configurations, each capable of realizing functional observers of different orders. This flexibility is important since a functional observer whose order equals the number of estimated functionals may not always exist. For each structure, algebraic existence conditions are established together with constructive synthesis procedures. A functional augmentation framework is developed to derive verifiable rank-based conditions for observers of various orders. In addition, the notion of generalized functionals, defined over an augmented delayed state vector, is introduced to provide greater flexibility in satisfying observer existence conditions and facilitating systematic design. Numerical examples illustrate the proposed theory.

AI.tldr: Functional observer design for linear time-delay systems with mismatched state and measurement delays, proposing three observer structures with algebraic existence conditions and constructive synthesis procedures.
AI.motivation: Existing observer designs assume instantaneous output availability, but practical systems often have measurement delays distinct from and not aligned with intrinsic state delays, creating a gap in functional estimation methods for such mismatched delay conditions.
AI.method: Three functional observer structures accommodating different delay configurations, with algebraic existence conditions and constructive synthesis procedures; functional augmentation framework for verifiable rank-based conditions; introduction of generalized functionals over augmented delayed state vector.
AI.result: Developed comprehensive framework for functional state estimation in linear time-delay systems with mismatched delays, providing flexible observer structures, existence conditions, and systematic design procedures validated through numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach successfully addresses functional estimation for linear time-delay systems with distinct state and measurement delays, offering flexible observer structures and systematic design methods that overcome limitations of existing approaches.

title: Acoustic and Semantic Modeling of Emotion in Spoken Language
summary: Emotions play a central role in human communication, shaping trust, engagement, and social interaction. As artificial intelligence systems powered by large language models become increasingly integrated into everyday life, enabling them to reliably understand and generate human emotions remains an important challenge. While emotional expression is inherently multimodal, this thesis focuses on emotions conveyed through spoken language and investigates how acoustic and semantic information can be jointly modeled to advance both emotion understanding and emotion synthesis from speech. The first part of the thesis studies emotion-aware representation learning through pre-training. We propose strategies that incorporate acoustic and semantic supervision to learn representations that better capture affective cues in speech. A speech-driven supervised pre-training framework is also introduced to enable large-scale emotion-aware text modeling without requiring manually annotated text corpora. The second part addresses emotion recognition in conversational settings. Hierarchical architectures combining cross-modal attention and mixture-of-experts fusion are developed to integrate acoustic and semantic information across conversational turns. Finally, the thesis introduces a textless and non-parallel speech-to-speech framework for emotion style transfer that enables controllable emotional transformations while preserving speaker identity and linguistic content. The results demonstrate improved emotion transfer and show that style-transferred speech can be used for data augmentation to improve emotion recognition.

AI.tldr: This thesis develops multimodal approaches for emotion understanding and synthesis from speech, focusing on acoustic-semantic joint modeling, conversational emotion recognition, and textless emotion style transfer.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more integrated into daily life, enabling reliable emotion understanding and generation remains crucial. While emotion expression is multimodal, this work focuses on spoken language to advance both emotion understanding and synthesis from speech.
AI.method: Three-part approach: 1) Emotion-aware representation learning through acoustic-semantic supervised pre-training; 2) Hierarchical architectures with cross-modal attention and mixture-of-experts fusion for conversational emotion recognition; 3) Textless, non-parallel speech-to-speech framework for emotion style transfer with speaker identity and content preservation.
AI.result: Improved emotion transfer capabilities and demonstration that style-transferred speech can be effectively used for data augmentation to enhance emotion recognition performance.
AI.conclusion: The thesis successfully advances emotion understanding and synthesis from speech through multimodal acoustic-semantic modeling, with applications in conversational emotion recognition and controllable emotion style transfer.

title: Gender Fairness in Audio Deepfake Detection: Performance and Disparity Analysis
summary: Audio deepfake detection aims to detect real human voices from those generated by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and has emerged as a significant problem in the field of voice biometrics systems. With the ever-improving quality of synthetic voice, the probability of such a voice being exploited for illicit practices like identity thest and impersonation increases. Although significant progress has been made in the field of Audio Deepfake Detection in recent times, the issue of gender bias remains underexplored and in its nascent stage In this paper, we have attempted a thorough analysis of gender dependent performance and fairness in audio deepfake detection models. We have used the ASVspoof 5 dataset and train a ResNet-18 classifier and evaluate detection performance across four different audio features, and compared the performance with baseline AASIST model. Beyond conventional metrics such as Equal Error Rate (EER %), we incorporated five established fairness metrics to quantify gender disparities in the model. Our results show that even when the overall EER difference between genders appears low, fairness-aware evaluation reveals disparities in error distribution that are obscured by aggregate performance measures. These findings demonstrate that reliance on standard metrics is unreliable, whereas fairness metrics provide critical insights into demographic-specific failure modes. This work highlights the importance of fairness-aware evaluation for developing a more equitable, robust, and trustworthy audio deepfake detection system.

AI.tldr: Analysis of gender bias in audio deepfake detection reveals that standard performance metrics like EER mask significant gender disparities, while fairness metrics uncover critical demographic-specific failure patterns in detection systems.
AI.motivation: Audio deepfake detection is crucial for voice biometrics security, but improving synthetic voice quality increases risks of identity theft and impersonation. Despite progress in detection, gender bias remains underexplored, creating potential vulnerabilities in equitable system performance.
AI.method: Used ASVspoof 5 dataset to train ResNet-18 classifier and evaluate across four audio features, comparing with baseline AASIST model. Employed both conventional metrics (EER %) and five established fairness metrics to quantify gender disparities in model performance.
AI.result: Results show that even with low overall EER differences between genders, fairness-aware evaluation reveals significant disparities in error distribution that are obscured by aggregate performance measures. Standard metrics prove unreliable for detecting demographic-specific failure modes.
AI.conclusion: Fairness metrics provide critical insights into gender disparities that standard metrics miss, highlighting the importance of fairness-aware evaluation for developing more equitable, robust, and trustworthy audio deepfake detection systems.

title: Distributionally robust two-stage model predictive control: adaptive constraint tightening with stability guarantee
summary: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is widely recognized for its ability to explicitly handle system constraints. In practice, system states are often affected by disturbances with unknown distributions. While robust MPC guarantees constraint satisfaction under worst-case scenarios, it tends to be overly conservative. Stochastic MPC balances conservatism and performance but relies on precise knowledge of the disturbance distribution, which is often unavailable. To address this challenge, this paper introduces Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) into the MPC framework and proposes a novel Two-Stage Distributionally Robust MPC (TSDR-MPC) scheme. The key innovation lies in formulating constraint violation penalties as a second-stage optimization problem, which, combined with the first-stage quadratic cost, constitutes a two-stage distributionally robust program. This structure enables adaptive constraint tightening against disturbances with unknown time-varying means and covariances. Utilizing a Wasserstein ambiguity set, we derive a tractable reformulation via strong duality and develop a cutting-plane algorithm that converges in a finite number of iterations, suitable for real-time implementation. To ensure closed-loop stability even under non-zero mean disturbances, we introduce a terminal constraint applied solely to the nominal system; this constraint is proportional to the current state and independent of distributional uncertainty, thus preserving overall feasibility. We provide rigorous theoretical guarantees, including recursive feasibility, finite-time algorithm termination, and an asymptotic performance bound on the average closed-loop cost. Numerical simulations validate the adaptability and robustness of the proposed framework under various disturbance scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes TSDR-MPC: a two-stage distributionally robust MPC framework using Wasserstein ambiguity sets to handle disturbances with unknown time-varying means/covariances, balancing robustness and performance while ensuring feasibility and stability.
AI.motivation: Standard MPC handles constraints but struggles with unknown disturbance distributions. Robust MPC is too conservative, stochastic MPC requires precise distribution knowledge. Need a framework that balances conservatism and performance without requiring exact disturbance distribution information.
AI.method: Introduces Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) into MPC with a two-stage structure: first-stage quadratic cost plus second-stage constraint violation penalties. Uses Wasserstein ambiguity sets for unknown time-varying disturbance means/covariances. Derives tractable reformulation via strong duality, develops cutting-plane algorithm for real-time implementation. Adds terminal constraint on nominal system for stability under non-zero mean disturbances.
AI.result: Provides rigorous theoretical guarantees: recursive feasibility, finite-time algorithm termination, asymptotic performance bound on average closed-loop cost. Numerical simulations demonstrate adaptability and robustness across various disturbance scenarios.
AI.conclusion: TSDR-MPC effectively bridges the gap between overly conservative robust MPC and distribution-dependent stochastic MPC, offering a practical solution for systems with unknown time-varying disturbance characteristics while maintaining theoretical guarantees and computational tractability.

title: StuPASE: Towards Low-Hallucination Studio-Quality Generative Speech Enhancement
summary: Achieving high perceptual quality without hallucination remains a challenge in generative speech enhancement (SE). A representative approach, PASE, is robust to hallucination but has limited perceptual quality under adverse conditions. We propose StuPASE, built upon PASE to achieve studio-level quality while retaining its low-hallucination property. First, we show that finetuning PASE with dry targets rather than targets containing simulated early reflections substantially improves dereverberation. Second, to address performance limitations under strong additive noise, we replace the GAN-based generative module in PASE with a flow-matching module, enabling studio-quality generation even under highly challenging conditions. Experiments demonstrate that StuPASE consistently produces perceptually high-quality speech while maintaining low hallucination, outperforming state-of-the-art SE methods. Audio demos are available at: https://xiaobin-rong.github.io/stupase_demo/.

AI.tldr: StuPASE improves upon PASE to achieve studio-level speech enhancement quality while maintaining low hallucination, using dry target finetuning and flow-matching instead of GANs.
AI.motivation: Existing generative speech enhancement methods face a trade-off between perceptual quality and hallucination; PASE is robust to hallucination but has limited perceptual quality under adverse conditions.
AI.method: Two key improvements: 1) Finetuning PASE with dry targets (without simulated early reflections) for better dereverberation; 2) Replacing the GAN-based generative module with a flow-matching module to handle strong additive noise and enable studio-quality generation.
AI.result: StuPASE consistently produces perceptually high-quality speech while maintaining low hallucination, outperforming state-of-the-art speech enhancement methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed StuPASE framework successfully achieves studio-level speech enhancement quality while preserving the low-hallucination property of PASE, addressing the quality-hallucination trade-off in generative speech enhancement.

title: The Costs of Reproducibility in Music Separation Research: a Replication of Band-Split RNN
summary: Music source separation is the task of isolating the instrumental tracks from a music song. Despite its spectacular recent progress, the trend towards more complex architectures and training protocols exacerbates reproducibility issues. The band-split recurrent neural networks (BSRNN) model is promising in this regard, since it yields close to state-of-the-art results on public datasets, and requires reasonable resources for training. Unfortunately, it is not straightforward to reproduce since its full code is not available. In this paper, we attempt to replicate BSRNN as closely as possible to the original paper through extensive experiments, which allows us to conduct a critical reflection on this reproducibility issue. Our contributions are three-fold. First, this study yields several insights on the model design and training pipeline, which sheds light on potential future improvements. In particular, since we were unsuccessful in reproducing the original results, we explore additional variants that ultimately yield an optimized BSRNN model, whose performance largely improves that of the original. Second, we discuss reproducibility issues from both methodological and practical perspectives. We notably underline how substantial time and energy costs could have been saved upon availability of the full pipeline. Third, our code and pre-trained models are released publicly to foster reproducible research. We hope that this study will contribute to spread awareness on the importance of reproducible research in the music separation community, and help promoting more transparent and sustainable practices.

AI.tldr: A reproducibility study of the BSRNN music source separation model that attempts to replicate original results, identifies issues, proposes improvements, and discusses reproducibility challenges in the field.
AI.motivation: To address reproducibility issues in music source separation research by attempting to replicate the BSRNN model, which shows promise but lacks available full code, and to critically reflect on reproducibility challenges in the field.
AI.method: Extensive experimental replication of the BSRNN model as closely as possible to the original paper, followed by exploration of additional variants to optimize the model when original results couldn't be reproduced.
AI.result: The study yields insights into model design and training pipeline, develops an optimized BSRNN variant with significantly improved performance over the original, and identifies substantial reproducibility challenges requiring extensive time and resources.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights critical reproducibility issues in music separation research, provides an improved BSRNN model with public code and pre-trained models, and advocates for more transparent and sustainable research practices in the community.

title: System-wide Dynamic Performance Metric for IBR-based Power Networks
summary: In power networks based on Inverter-Based Resources (IBRs), fast controllers cause frequency and voltage dynamics to overlap. Thus, it becomes critical to assess the overall dynamic performance of such networks through a combined system-wide metric. This letter presents a unified metric designed to evaluate dynamic performance in such cases. The proposed metric consists of a weighted sum of local voltage phasor variations at each bus, where the weights are the complex powers injected at the buses. The proposed metric is further decomposed into device-driven and network-driven components, enabling a more comprehensive assessment of grid dynamics. A case study based on a modified version of the IEEE 39-bus system is presented, in which synchronous machines are replaced by inverter-based resources. A sensitivity analysis of the R/X ratio is utilized to evaluate the metric in conventional grids, as well as in those characterized by strong voltage-frequency coupling with complex power flows.

AI.tldr: A unified metric for evaluating dynamic performance in IBR-dominated power networks, combining voltage phasor variations weighted by complex power injections, with decomposition into device-driven and network-driven components.
AI.motivation: In IBR-based power networks, fast controllers cause overlapping frequency and voltage dynamics, making it critical to assess overall dynamic performance through a combined system-wide metric rather than separate frequency and voltage analyses.
AI.method: Proposes a unified metric as a weighted sum of local voltage phasor variations at each bus, with weights being the complex powers injected at the buses. The metric is further decomposed into device-driven and network-driven components for comprehensive assessment.
AI.result: Case study using modified IEEE 39-bus system with synchronous machines replaced by IBRs demonstrates the metric's application. Sensitivity analysis of R/X ratio evaluates metric performance in both conventional grids and those with strong voltage-frequency coupling and complex power flows.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unified metric enables comprehensive assessment of dynamic performance in IBR-dominated power networks by capturing combined voltage-frequency dynamics and distinguishing between device-level and network-level contributions to system behavior.

title: End-to-End Direction-Aware Keyword Spotting with Spatial Priors in Noisy Environments
summary: Keyword spotting (KWS) is crucial for many speech-driven applications, but robust KWS in noisy environments remains challenging. Conventional systems often rely on single-channel inputs and a cascaded pipeline separating front-end enhancement from KWS. This precludes joint optimization, inherently limiting performance. We present an end-to-end multi-channel KWS framework that exploits spatial cues to improve noise robustness. A spatial encoder learns inter-channel features, while a spatial embedding injects directional priors; the fused representation is processed by a streaming backbone. Experiments in simulated noisy conditions across multiple signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) show that spatial modeling and directional priors each yield clear gains over baselines, with their combination achieving the best results. These findings validate end-to-end multi-channel spatial modeling, indicating strong potential for the target-speaker-aware detection in complex acoustic scenarios.

AI.tldr: End-to-end multi-channel keyword spotting framework using spatial modeling and directional priors for improved noise robustness.
AI.motivation: Robust keyword spotting in noisy environments is challenging; conventional single-channel cascaded systems prevent joint optimization and limit performance.
AI.method: End-to-end multi-channel framework with spatial encoder for inter-channel features, spatial embedding for directional priors, and streaming backbone for fused representation processing.
AI.result: Spatial modeling and directional priors each provide clear gains over baselines; their combination achieves best results across multiple SNRs in simulated noisy conditions.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end multi-channel spatial modeling validates strong potential for target-speaker-aware detection in complex acoustic scenarios.

title: How Contrastive Decoding Enhances Large Audio Language Models?
comment: Submitted to INTERSPEECH 2026. Code and additional analysis results are provided in our repository: https://github.com/nervjack2/LALM-Contrastive-Decoding-Error-Profiles
summary: While Contrastive Decoding (CD) has proven effective at enhancing Large Audio Language Models (LALMs), the underlying mechanisms driving its success and the comparative efficacy of different strategies remain unclear. This study systematically evaluates four distinct CD strategies across diverse LALM architectures. We identify Audio-Aware Decoding and Audio Contrastive Decoding as the most effective methods. However, their impact varies significantly by model. To explain this variability, we introduce a Transition Matrix framework to map error pattern shifts during inference. Our analysis demonstrates that CD reliably rectifies errors in which models falsely claim an absence of audio or resort to uncertainty-driven guessing. Conversely, it fails to correct flawed reasoning or confident misassertions. Ultimately, these findings provide a clear guideline for determining which LALM architectures are most suitable for CD enhancement based on their baseline error profiles.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation of four Contrastive Decoding strategies for Large Audio Language Models reveals Audio-Aware and Audio Contrastive Decoding as most effective, with performance varying by model architecture. A Transition Matrix framework explains error pattern shifts, showing CD corrects false-negative and uncertainty errors but not reasoning flaws.
AI.motivation: Despite Contrastive Decoding's proven effectiveness for Large Audio Language Models, the underlying mechanisms and comparative efficacy of different CD strategies remain unclear, necessitating systematic evaluation to understand when and why CD works.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated four distinct CD strategies across diverse LALM architectures, introduced a Transition Matrix framework to map error pattern shifts during inference, and analyzed error correction patterns across different model types.
AI.result: Audio-Aware Decoding and Audio Contrastive Decoding identified as most effective CD strategies, but impact varies significantly by model. CD reliably corrects errors where models falsely claim absence of audio or resort to uncertainty-driven guessing, but fails to correct flawed reasoning or confident misassertions.
AI.conclusion: Findings provide clear guidelines for determining which LALM architectures are most suitable for CD enhancement based on their baseline error profiles, enabling targeted application of CD strategies to maximize performance improvements.

title: Vector-field guided constraint-following control for path following of uncertain mechanical systems
summary: This note proposes a general control approach, called vector-field guided constraint-following control, to solve the dynamics control problem of geometric path-following for a class of uncertain mechanical systems. More specifically, it operates at the dynamics level and can handle both fully-actuated and underactuated mechanical systems, heterogeneous (possibly fast) time-varying uncertainties with unknown bounds, and geometric desired paths that may be self-intersecting. Simulations are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach.

AI.tldr: A vector-field guided constraint-following control method for geometric path-following of uncertain mechanical systems, handling both fully/underactuated systems, time-varying uncertainties with unknown bounds, and self-intersecting paths.
AI.motivation: To address the dynamics control problem of geometric path-following for uncertain mechanical systems, particularly handling challenges like time-varying uncertainties with unknown bounds, underactuation, and complex path geometries including self-intersecting paths.
AI.method: Vector-field guided constraint-following control operating at the dynamics level, designed to handle both fully-actuated and underactuated mechanical systems, heterogeneous time-varying uncertainties with unknown bounds, and geometric desired paths that may be self-intersecting.
AI.result: Simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, showing successful geometric path-following control for the targeted class of uncertain mechanical systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed vector-field guided constraint-following control provides a general solution for geometric path-following of uncertain mechanical systems, capable of handling various challenging conditions including underactuation, unknown bounded uncertainties, and complex path geometries.

title: A Fast Solver for Interpolating Stochastic Differential Equation Diffusion Models for Speech Restoration
summary: Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) are a well-established class of diffusion models for unconditional image generation, while SGMSE+ is a well-established conditional diffusion model for speech enhancement. One of the downsides of diffusion models is that solving the reverse process requires many evaluations of a large Neural Network. Although advanced fast sampling solvers have been developed for DPMs, they are not directly applicable to models such as SGMSE+ due to differences in their diffusion processes. Specifically, DPMs transform between the data distribution and a standard Gaussian distribution, whereas SGMSE+ interpolates between the target distribution and a noisy observation. This work first develops a formalism of interpolating Stochastic Differential Equations (iSDEs) that includes SGMSE+, and second proposes a solver for iSDEs. The proposed solver enables fast sampling with as few as 10 Neural Network evaluations across multiple speech restoration tasks.

AI.tldr: A fast sampling solver for interpolating Stochastic Differential Equations (iSDEs) that enables efficient speech enhancement with only 10 neural network evaluations, bridging the gap between unconditional DPMs and conditional models like SGMSE+.
AI.motivation: Existing fast sampling solvers for Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) cannot be directly applied to conditional diffusion models like SGMSE+ due to fundamental differences in their diffusion processes. DPMs transform between data and Gaussian distributions, while SGMSE+ interpolates between target distribution and noisy observations, creating a need for specialized fast sampling methods for conditional diffusion models.
AI.method: 1) Developed a formalism of interpolating Stochastic Differential Equations (iSDEs) that includes SGMSE+ as a special case. 2) Proposed a novel solver specifically designed for iSDEs that enables fast sampling by reducing the number of neural network evaluations required during the reverse process.
AI.result: The proposed iSDE solver achieves fast sampling with as few as 10 neural network evaluations across multiple speech restoration tasks, significantly reducing computational cost compared to traditional diffusion model sampling while maintaining performance.
AI.conclusion: The iSDE formalism and corresponding solver successfully bridge the gap between unconditional and conditional diffusion models, enabling efficient fast sampling for speech enhancement applications while maintaining the quality benefits of diffusion-based approaches.

title: Paralinguistic Emotion-Aware Validation Timing Detection in Japanese Empathetic Spoken Dialogue
summary: Emotional Validation is a psychotherapy communication technique that involves recognizing, understanding, and explicitly acknowledging another person's feelings and actions, which strengthens alliance and reduces negative affect. To maximize the emotional support provided by validation, it is crucial to deliver it with appropriate timing and frequency. This study investigates validation timing detection from the speech perspective. Leveraging both paralinguistic and emotional information, we propose a paralinguistic- and emotion-aware model for validation timing detection without relying on textual context. Specifically, we first conduct continued self-supervised training and fine-tuning on different HuBERT backbones to obtain (i) a paralinguistics-aware Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) encoder and (ii) a multi-task speech emotion classification encoder. We then fuse these encoders and further fine-tune the combined model on the downstream validation timing detection task. Experimental evaluations on the TUT Emotional Storytelling Corpus (TESC) compare multiple models, fusion mechanisms, and training strategies, and demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves significant improvements over conventional speech baselines. Our results indicate that non-linguistic speech cues, when integrated with affect-related representations, carry sufficient signal to decide when validation should be expressed, offering a speech-first pathway toward more empathetic human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: A speech-based approach for detecting optimal timing of emotional validation in conversations using paralinguistic and emotional cues without textual context, achieving improved performance over conventional speech baselines.
AI.motivation: Emotional validation is crucial for effective psychotherapy and emotional support, but its timing and frequency significantly impact its effectiveness. Current approaches may rely on textual context, but this study aims to detect validation timing purely from speech cues to enable more empathetic human-robot interactions.
AI.method: Proposes a paralinguistic- and emotion-aware model that combines: (1) a paralinguistics-aware SSL encoder via continued self-supervised training and fine-tuning on HuBERT backbones, (2) a multi-task speech emotion classification encoder, and (3) fusion of these encoders with further fine-tuning on validation timing detection. Evaluated on TUT Emotional Storytelling Corpus with multiple models, fusion mechanisms, and training strategies.
AI.result: The proposed approach achieves significant improvements over conventional speech baselines. Results demonstrate that non-linguistic speech cues integrated with affect-related representations provide sufficient signal to determine when validation should be expressed.
AI.conclusion: Non-linguistic speech cues combined with emotional representations can effectively detect validation timing without textual context, offering a speech-first pathway toward more empathetic human-robot interaction by enabling appropriate timing of emotional validation responses.

title: Constrained finite-time stabilization by model predictive control: an infinite control horizon framework
summary: Existing results on finite-time model predictive control (MPC) often rely on terminal equality constraint, switching inside one-step region, or terminal cost with short control horizon, leading to limited initial feasibility. This paper proposes an infinite-horizon Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for the constrained finite-time stabilization of discrete-time systems, overcoming limitations found in existing finite-time MPC results. The proposed framework is built upon a terminal cost strategy, but expands it by replacing the short-horizon terminal cost with the sum of stage costs over an infinite control horizon. This design choice significantly enlarges the initial feasibility region and avoids the need for terminal equality constraints or switching strategies during implementation. It is proved that the proposed finite-time MPC guarantees finite-time stabilization performance once the state trajectory enters the predefined terminal set. The infinite-horizon finite-time MPC is shown to be equivalently implementable as a finite-horizon MPC with a terminal cost, thereby ensuring computational tractability. The proposed finite-time MPC is systematically extended and shown to be applicable to both constrained multi-input linear systems and a class of constrained nonlinear systems that are feedback linearizable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an infinite-horizon MPC framework for finite-time stabilization that replaces short-horizon terminal costs with infinite-horizon stage cost sums, significantly expanding feasibility regions while maintaining computational tractability through equivalent finite-horizon implementation.
AI.motivation: Existing finite-time MPC approaches suffer from limited initial feasibility due to reliance on terminal equality constraints, switching strategies, or short-horizon terminal costs. There is a need for MPC frameworks that overcome these limitations while maintaining computational tractability.
AI.method: Proposes an infinite-horizon MPC framework using a terminal cost strategy where the terminal cost is replaced by the sum of stage costs over an infinite control horizon. This is shown to be equivalently implementable as a finite-horizon MPC with terminal cost, ensuring computational tractability. The method is extended to constrained multi-input linear systems and feedback-linearizable nonlinear systems.
AI.result: The proposed framework significantly enlarges the initial feasibility region compared to existing finite-time MPC methods, avoids terminal equality constraints and switching strategies, guarantees finite-time stabilization once states enter the terminal set, and maintains computational tractability through equivalent finite-horizon implementation.
AI.conclusion: The infinite-horizon MPC framework successfully overcomes limitations of existing finite-time MPC methods by expanding feasibility regions while maintaining computational tractability, with applicability demonstrated for both constrained linear systems and feedback-linearizable nonlinear systems.

title: Speech-Omni-Lite: Portable Speech Interfaces for Vision-Language Models
summary: While large-scale omni-models have demonstrated impressive capabilities across various modalities, their strong performance heavily relies on massive multimodal data and incurs substantial computational costs. This work introduces Speech-Omni-Lite, a cost-efficient framework for extending pre-trained Visual-Language (VL) backbones with speech understanding and generation capabilities, while fully preserving the backbones' vision-language performance. Specifically, the VL backbone is equipped with two lightweight, trainable plug-and-play modules, a speech projector and a speech token generator, while keeping the VL backbone fully frozen. To mitigate the scarcity of spoken QA corpora, a low-cost data construction strategy is proposed to generate Question-Text Answer-Text-Speech (QTATS) data from existing ASR speech-text pairs, facilitating effective speech generation training. Experimental results show that, even with only thousands of hours of speech training data, Speech-Omni-Lite achieves excellent spoken QA performance, which is comparable to omni-models trained on millions of hours of speech data. Furthermore, the learned speech modules exhibit strong transferability across VL backbones.

AI.tldr: Speech-Omni-Lite is a lightweight framework that extends pre-trained visual-language models to handle speech understanding and generation with minimal computational cost, using only thousands of hours of speech data instead of millions.
AI.motivation: Existing large-scale omni-models require massive multimodal data and substantial computational resources, creating a need for more cost-efficient approaches that can add speech capabilities to pre-trained models without compromising existing vision-language performance.
AI.method: Uses two lightweight plug-and-play modules (speech projector and speech token generator) attached to a frozen VL backbone. Introduces a low-cost data construction strategy to generate QTATS (Question-Text Answer-Text-Speech) data from existing ASR speech-text pairs for training.
AI.result: Achieves spoken QA performance comparable to omni-models trained on millions of hours of speech data, using only thousands of hours. The learned speech modules show strong transferability across different VL backbones.
AI.conclusion: Speech-Omni-Lite provides an efficient, cost-effective solution for extending pre-trained VL models with speech capabilities while preserving original performance, addressing data scarcity and computational cost challenges in multimodal learning.

title: TimberAgent: Gram-Guided Retrieval for Executable Music Effect Control
summary: Digital audio workstations expose rich effect chains, yet a semantic gap remains between perceptual user intent and low-level signal-processing parameters. We study retrieval-grounded audio effect control, where the output is an editable plugin configuration rather than a finalized waveform. Our focus is Texture Resonance Retrieval (TRR), an audio representation built from Gram matrices of projected mid-level Wav2Vec2 activations. This design preserves texture-relevant co-activation structure. We evaluate TRR on a guitar-effects benchmark with 1,063 candidate presets and 204 queries. The evaluation follows Protocol-A, a cross-validation scheme that prevents train-test leakage. We compare TRR against CLAP and internal retrieval baselines (Wav2Vec-RAG, Text-RAG, FeatureNN-RAG), using min-max normalized metrics grounded in physical DSP parameter ranges. Ablation studies validate TRR's core design choices: projection dimensionality, layer selection, and projection type. A near-duplicate sensitivity analysis confirms that results are robust to trivial knowledge-base matches. TRR achieves the lowest normalized parameter error among evaluated methods. A multiple-stimulus listening study with 26 participants provides complementary perceptual evidence. We interpret these results as benchmark evidence that texture-aware retrieval is useful for editable audio effect control, while broader personalization and real-audio robustness claims remain outside the verified evidence presented here.

AI.tldr: Texture Resonance Retrieval (TRR) is a novel audio representation for retrieval-grounded audio effect control that achieves lowest normalized parameter error on a guitar-effects benchmark by preserving texture-relevant co-activation structure through Gram matrices of projected Wav2Vec2 activations.
AI.motivation: There exists a semantic gap between perceptual user intent and low-level signal-processing parameters in digital audio workstations. The paper aims to bridge this gap through retrieval-grounded audio effect control that outputs editable plugin configurations rather than finalized waveforms.
AI.method: Proposes Texture Resonance Retrieval (TRR), an audio representation built from Gram matrices of projected mid-level Wav2Vec2 activations. This design preserves texture-relevant co-activation structure. Evaluated on a guitar-effects benchmark with 1,063 candidate presets and 204 queries using Protocol-A cross-validation to prevent train-test leakage. Compared against CLAP and internal retrieval baselines (Wav2Vec-RAG, Text-RAG, FeatureNN-RAG) with min-max normalized metrics grounded in physical DSP parameter ranges.
AI.result: TRR achieves the lowest normalized parameter error among evaluated methods. Ablation studies validate TRR's core design choices: projection dimensionality, layer selection, and projection type. Near-duplicate sensitivity analysis confirms robustness to trivial knowledge-base matches. A multiple-stimulus listening study with 26 participants provides complementary perceptual evidence.
AI.conclusion: The results provide benchmark evidence that texture-aware retrieval is useful for editable audio effect control. However, broader claims about personalization and real-audio robustness remain outside the verified evidence presented in this study.

title: Embedded Model Predictive Control for EMS-type Maglev Vehicles
summary: Current developments of high-speed magnetic levitation technology using the principle of the electromagnet suspension (EMS) focus on reaching vehicle speeds of more than 600 km/h. With increasing vehicle speeds, however, updated control algorithms need to be investigated to reliably stabilize the system and meet the demands in terms of ride comfort. This article examines the modern and popular approach of model predictive control and its application to the magnetic levitation control system. Investigated key aspects are the parameterization of the model predictive controller and its implementation on embedded, resource constrained hardware. The results reveal that model predictive control is capable to robustly stabilize the highly nonlinear and constrained system even at very high speed. Furthermore, processor-in-the-loop studies are carried out to validate the designed control algorithms on a microcontroller.

AI.tldr: Model predictive control (MPC) is applied to high-speed magnetic levitation systems (>600 km/h) for robust stabilization and ride comfort, with implementation validated on embedded hardware.
AI.motivation: As high-speed magnetic levitation (EMS) technology targets speeds exceeding 600 km/h, existing control algorithms need enhancement to ensure reliable system stabilization and meet ride comfort requirements. The highly nonlinear and constrained nature of the system demands advanced control approaches.
AI.method: The paper investigates model predictive control (MPC) for magnetic levitation systems, focusing on controller parameterization and implementation on resource-constrained embedded hardware. Processor-in-the-loop (PIL) studies are conducted to validate the control algorithms on a microcontroller.
AI.result: Model predictive control demonstrates capability to robustly stabilize the highly nonlinear and constrained magnetic levitation system even at very high speeds. The PIL studies successfully validate the designed control algorithms on microcontroller hardware.
AI.conclusion: MPC is a viable and effective control approach for high-speed magnetic levitation systems, providing robust stabilization while addressing implementation challenges on embedded hardware platforms.

title: Finetuning a Text-to-Audio Model for Room Impulse Response Generation
summary: Room Impulse Responses (RIRs) enable realistic acoustic simulation, with applications ranging from multimedia production to speech data augmentation. However, acquiring high-quality real-world RIRs is labor-intensive, and data scarcity remains a challenge for data-driven RIR generation approaches. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to RIR generation by fine-tuning a pre-trained text-to-audio model, demonstrating for the first time that large-scale generative audio priors can be effectively leveraged for the task. To address the lack of text-RIR paired data, we establish a labeling pipeline utilizing vision-language models to extract acoustic descriptions from existing image-RIR datasets. We introduce an in-context learning strategy to accommodate free-form user prompts during inference. Evaluations involving MUSHRA listening tests and downstream ASR performance demonstrate that our model generates plausible RIRs and serves as an effective tool for speech data augmentation.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning a pre-trained text-to-audio model for Room Impulse Response generation using vision-language models to create text-RIR pairs and in-context learning for free-form prompts.
AI.motivation: Real-world RIR acquisition is labor-intensive, creating data scarcity that limits data-driven RIR generation approaches, despite RIRs being essential for realistic acoustic simulation in multimedia production and speech data augmentation.
AI.method: Fine-tune pre-trained text-to-audio model for RIR generation; use vision-language models to extract acoustic descriptions from existing image-RIR datasets to create text-RIR pairs; implement in-context learning strategy for free-form user prompts during inference.
AI.result: Model generates plausible RIRs as demonstrated by MUSHRA listening tests; serves as effective tool for speech data augmentation with improved downstream ASR performance.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale generative audio priors can be effectively leveraged for RIR generation, overcoming data scarcity through innovative labeling pipeline and in-context learning, enabling realistic acoustic simulation applications.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Engine Sound Modeling with Differentiable Pulse-Train Synthesis
comment: Preprint. 5 pages, 2 figures. Audio examples, code, and model weights available online
summary:
Engine sounds originate from sequential exhaust pressure pulses rather than sustained harmonic oscillations. While neural synthesis methods typically aim to approximate the resulting spectral characteristics, we propose directly modeling the underlying pulse shapes and temporal structure. We present the Pulse-Train-Resonator (PTR) model, a differentiable synthesis architecture that generates engine audio as parameterized pulse trains aligned to engine firing patterns and propagates them through recursive Karplus-Strong resonators simulating exhaust acoustics. The architecture integrates physics-informed inductive biases including harmonic decay, thermodynamic pitch modulation, valve-dynamics envelopes, exhaust system resonances and derived engine operating modes such as throttle operation and deceleration fuel cutoff (DCFO).
  Validated on three diverse engine types totaling 7.5 hours of audio, PTR achieves a 21% improvement in harmonic reconstruction and a 5.7% reduction in total loss over a harmonic-plus-noise baseline model, while providing interpretable parameters corresponding to physical phenomena.
  Complete code, model weights, and audio examples are openly available.

AI.tldr: PTR model generates engine sounds by modeling exhaust pressure pulses through parameterized pulse trains and recursive resonators, achieving better harmonic reconstruction than harmonic-plus-noise baselines.
AI.motivation: Engine sounds originate from sequential exhaust pressure pulses rather than sustained harmonic oscillations, but current neural synthesis methods typically only approximate spectral characteristics rather than modeling the underlying physical structure.
AI.method: Pulse-Train-Resonator (PTR) model: differentiable synthesis architecture that generates engine audio as parameterized pulse trains aligned to engine firing patterns, propagated through recursive Karplus-Strong resonators simulating exhaust acoustics with physics-informed inductive biases.
AI.result: Validated on three diverse engine types (7.5 hours total), PTR achieves 21% improvement in harmonic reconstruction and 5.7% reduction in total loss over harmonic-plus-noise baseline, while providing interpretable parameters corresponding to physical phenomena.
AI.conclusion: PTR successfully models engine sounds by directly simulating the underlying physical pulse structure rather than approximating spectral characteristics, offering both improved performance and interpretable physical parameters.

title: Learning-Augmented Primal-Dual Control Design for Secondary Frequency Regulation
summary: Frequency stability is fundamental to the secure operation of power systems. With growing uncertainty and volatility introduced by renewable generation, secondary frequency regulation must now deliver enhanced performance not only in the steady state but also during transients. This paper presents a systematic framework to embed learning in the design of a primal-dual controller that provides provable (potentially exponential) stability and steady-state optimality, while simultaneously improving key transient metrics, including frequency nadir and control effort, in a data-driven manner. In particular, we employ the primal-dual dynamics of an optimization problem that encodes steady-state objectives to realize secondary frequency control with asymptotic stability guarantee. To augment transient performance of the controller via learning, a change of variables on control inputs, which will be deployed by neural networks, is proposed such that under mild conditions, stability and steady-state optimality are preserved. It further allows us to define a learning goal that accounts for the exponential convergence rate, frequency nadir and accumulated control effort, and use sample trajectories to enhance these metrics. Simulation results validate the theories and demonstrate superior transient performance of the learning-augmented primal-dual controller.

AI.tldr: Learning-augmented primal-dual controller for secondary frequency regulation that guarantees stability and optimality while improving transient performance through data-driven neural network adaptation.
AI.motivation: Increasing uncertainty and volatility from renewable generation requires enhanced secondary frequency regulation performance in both steady-state and transient conditions, beyond traditional control approaches.
AI.method: Primal-dual dynamics from optimization encoding steady-state objectives for secondary frequency control, augmented with neural networks through a change of variables that preserves stability and optimality while learning to improve transient metrics.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees of stability (potentially exponential) and steady-state optimality, with demonstrated superior transient performance including improved frequency nadir and reduced control effort in simulations.
AI.conclusion: A systematic framework successfully integrates learning with primal-dual control to enhance transient performance while maintaining provable stability and optimality for modern power systems with high renewable penetration.

title: A Semi-spontaneous Dutch Speech Dataset for Speech Enhancement and Speech Recognition
summary: We present DRES: a 1.5-hour Dutch realistic elicited (semi-spontaneous) speech dataset from 80 speakers recorded in noisy, public indoor environments. DRES was designed as a test set for the evaluation of state-of-the-art (SOTA) automatic speech recognition (ASR) and speech enhancement (SE) models in a real-world scenario: a person speaking in a public indoor space with background talkers and noise. The speech was recorded with a four-channel linear microphone array. In this work we evaluate the speech quality of five well-known single-channel SE algorithms and the recognition performance of eight SOTA off-the-shelf ASR models before and after applying SE on the speech of DRES. We found that five out of the eight ASR models have WERs lower than 22% on DRES, despite the challenging conditions. In contrast to recent work, we did not find a positive effect of modern single-channel SE on ASR performance, emphasizing the importance of evaluating in realistic conditions.

AI.tldr: DRES is a 1.5-hour Dutch realistic speech dataset recorded in noisy public indoor environments, used to evaluate SOTA ASR and speech enhancement models, finding no positive effect of modern single-channel SE on ASR performance in realistic conditions.
AI.motivation: To create a realistic test set for evaluating state-of-the-art ASR and speech enhancement models in real-world scenarios where people speak in noisy public indoor spaces with background talkers and noise, addressing the gap between controlled lab evaluations and practical applications.
AI.method: Created DRES dataset with 80 speakers recorded in noisy public indoor environments using a four-channel linear microphone array. Evaluated five single-channel speech enhancement algorithms and eight SOTA off-the-shelf ASR models before and after applying SE on DRES speech.
AI.result: Five out of eight ASR models achieved WERs lower than 22% on DRES despite challenging conditions. Contrary to recent work, no positive effect of modern single-channel speech enhancement on ASR performance was found, highlighting the importance of realistic evaluation conditions.
AI.conclusion: Realistic evaluation conditions reveal limitations of current single-channel speech enhancement techniques for ASR improvement, emphasizing the need for datasets like DRES that better represent real-world scenarios and challenge the assumption that SE always benefits ASR performance.

title: MUGEN: Evaluating and Improving Multi-audio Understanding of Large Audio-Language Models
summary: While multi-audio understanding is critical for large audio-language models (LALMs), it remains underexplored. We introduce MUGEN, a comprehensive benchmark evaluating this capability across speech, general audio, and music. Our experiments reveal consistent weaknesses in multi-audio settings, and performance degrades sharply as the number of concurrent audio inputs increases, identifying input scaling as a fundamental bottleneck.
