We further investigate training-free strategies and observe that Audio-Permutational Self-Consistency, which diversifies the order of audio candidates, helps models form more robust aggregated predictions, yielding up to 6.28% accuracy gains. Combining this permutation strategy with Chain-of-Thought further improves performance to 6.74%. These results expose blind spots in current LALMs and provide a foundation for evaluating complex auditory comprehension.

AI.tldr: MUGEN benchmark reveals multi-audio understanding weaknesses in LALMs, with performance degrading as audio inputs increase; Audio-Permutational Self-Consistency and Chain-of-Thought improve accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multi-audio understanding is critical for large audio-language models but remains underexplored, creating a need for comprehensive evaluation of this capability across speech, general audio, and music domains.
AI.method: Introduces MUGEN benchmark for evaluating multi-audio understanding; investigates training-free strategies including Audio-Permutational Self-Consistency (diversifying audio order) and Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
AI.result: Reveals consistent weaknesses in multi-audio settings with sharp performance degradation as concurrent audio inputs increase; Audio-Permutational Self-Consistency yields up to 6.28% accuracy gains; combining with Chain-of-Thought improves to 6.74%.
AI.conclusion: Exposes blind spots in current LALMs regarding multi-audio understanding, identifies input scaling as fundamental bottleneck, and provides foundation for evaluating complex auditory comprehension with effective training-free strategies.

title: Field Free Novel Architecture for Spintronic Flash Analog to Digital Converter
comment: 9 pages incluinding 2 pages of reference, 11 figures and 2 tables. Invited and presented at conference(ICMAGMA,2024)
summary: A 3 bit Analog to Digital Converter (ADC) is designed using perpendicular Spin Orbit Torque Magnetic Tunnel Junction (SOT MTJ). A sampled analog input signal is transmitted as a spin orbit torque current (Iin) to a perpendicular SOT MTJ, and deterministic switching is supported by the Voltage Controlled Magnetic Anisotropy (VCMA) and Spin Transfer Torque (STT) switching methods. Analog to digital conversion is done by comparing input signal with varied critical current of SOT MTJs. The critical current of each is SOT MTJ governed by varying widths of Heavy Metal (HM). In the 3 bit ADC, there are two sets of 7 SOT MTJs for quantizing input value, a conversion set and dummy set for comparing the change in resistance state. As input signal passed through conversion set SOT MTJs switches from Parallel (P) to AntiParallel (AP) state if the input signal exceeds its critical current. The conversion set change in state is converted to thermometer codes by StrongARM latch comparator by comparing the resistance with dummy set SOT MTJs, where all the in P state or low resistance. A novel architecture is proposed for increasing speed of throughput, by utilizing the dummy set of as a conversion set and conversion set as dummy set, thus eliminating the reset step from analog to digital conversion. And by improving SOT-MTJ and timing blocks a field free spin flash ADC has a power consumption of 476 uW with a conversion rate of 304.1 MHz is produced.

AI.tldr: A 3-bit ADC using perpendicular SOT-MTJ devices achieves 304.1 MHz conversion rate with 476 μW power consumption through a novel architecture that eliminates reset steps by alternating conversion and dummy sets.
AI.motivation: To develop a high-speed, low-power analog-to-digital converter using spintronic devices (SOT-MTJs) that can overcome limitations of conventional CMOS ADCs, particularly by eliminating reset steps to increase throughput.
AI.method: Uses perpendicular SOT-MTJs with deterministic switching via VCMA and STT. Critical currents are varied by adjusting heavy metal widths. A 3-bit ADC employs two sets of 7 SOT-MTJs each (conversion and dummy sets). Input signal comparison triggers P→AP switching when exceeding critical current. StrongARM latch comparators convert resistance changes to thermometer codes. Novel architecture alternates conversion/dummy roles to eliminate reset steps.
AI.result: Achieves 304.1 MHz conversion rate with 476 μW power consumption. The field-free spin flash ADC demonstrates high-speed operation through the elimination of reset steps in the conversion cycle.
AI.conclusion: The SOT-MTJ based ADC architecture successfully eliminates reset steps by alternating conversion and dummy sets, enabling high-speed (304.1 MHz) operation with low power consumption (476 μW), demonstrating the potential of spintronic devices for high-performance analog-to-digital conversion.

title: Distributed Multichannel Wiener Filtering for Wireless Acoustic Sensor Networks
summary: In a wireless acoustic sensor network (WASN), devices (i.e., nodes) can collaborate through distributed algorithms to collectively perform audio signal processing tasks. This paper focuses on the distributed estimation of node-specific desired speech signals using network-wide Wiener filtering. The objective is to match the performance of a centralized system that would have access to all microphone signals, while reducing the communication bandwidth usage of the algorithm. Existing solutions, such as the distributed adaptive node-specific signal estimation (DANSE) algorithm, converge towards the multichannel Wiener filter (MWF) which solves a centralized linear minimum mean square error (LMMSE) signal estimation problem. However, they do so iteratively, which can be slow and impractical. Many solutions also assume that all nodes observe the same set of sources of interest, which is often not the case in practice. To overcome these limitations, we propose the distributed multichannel Wiener filter (dMWF) for fully connected WASNs. The dMWF is non-iterative and optimal even when nodes observe different sets of sources. In this algorithm, nodes exchange neighbor-pair-specific, low-dimensional (fused) signals estimating the contribution of sources observed by both nodes in the pair. We formally prove the optimality of dMWF and demonstrate its performance in simulated speech enhancement experiments. The proposed algorithm is shown to outperform DANSE in terms of objective metrics after short operation times, highlighting the benefit of its iterationless design.

AI.tldr: Proposes a non-iterative distributed multichannel Wiener filter (dMWF) for wireless acoustic sensor networks that achieves centralized optimal performance while reducing communication bandwidth, outperforming existing iterative methods like DANSE.
AI.motivation: Existing distributed algorithms for node-specific speech signal estimation in WASNs have limitations: they are iterative (slow convergence), assume all nodes observe the same sources (unrealistic in practice), and may not match centralized optimal performance while maintaining low communication bandwidth.
AI.method: Proposes distributed multichannel Wiener filter (dMWF) for fully connected WASNs. Nodes exchange neighbor-pair-specific, low-dimensional fused signals that estimate contributions of sources observed by both nodes in each pair. The algorithm is non-iterative and handles scenarios where nodes observe different source sets.
AI.result: Formally proves dMWF optimality (matches centralized MWF performance). Simulation experiments show dMWF outperforms DANSE in objective metrics after short operation times, demonstrating benefits of iterationless design.
AI.conclusion: dMWF provides a non-iterative, optimal distributed solution for node-specific speech signal estimation in WASNs that handles heterogeneous source observations, reduces communication bandwidth, and outperforms existing iterative methods in practical scenarios.

title: EmoSURA: Towards Accurate Evaluation of Detailed and Long-Context Emotional Speech Captions
summary: Recent advancements in speech captioning models have enabled the generation of rich, fine-grained captions for emotional speech. However, the evaluation of such captions remains a critical bottleneck: traditional N-gram metrics fail to capture semantic nuances, while LLM judges often suffer from reasoning inconsistency and context-collapse when processing long-form descriptions. In this work, we propose EmoSURA, a novel evaluation framework that shifts the paradigm from holistic scoring to atomic verification. EmoSURA decomposes complex captions into Atomic Perceptual Units, which are self-contained statements regarding vocal or emotional attributes, and employs an audio-grounded verification mechanism to validate each unit against the raw speech signal. Furthermore, we address the scarcity of standardized evaluation resources by introducing SURABench, a carefully balanced and stratified benchmark. Our experiments show that EmoSURA achieves a positive correlation with human judgments, offering a more reliable assessment for long-form captions compared to traditional metrics, which demonstrated negative correlations due to their sensitivity to caption length.

AI.tldr: EmoSURA is a novel evaluation framework for emotional speech captions that uses atomic verification of decomposed caption units against audio signals, addressing limitations of traditional metrics and LLM judges.
AI.motivation: Traditional N-gram metrics fail to capture semantic nuances in emotional speech captions, while LLM judges suffer from reasoning inconsistency and context-collapse when processing long-form descriptions, creating a critical bottleneck in evaluating speech captioning models.
AI.method: EmoSURA shifts from holistic scoring to atomic verification by decomposing complex captions into Atomic Perceptual Units (self-contained statements about vocal/emotional attributes) and employs audio-grounded verification to validate each unit against raw speech signals. Also introduces SURABench benchmark.
AI.result: EmoSURA achieves positive correlation with human judgments, offering more reliable assessment for long-form captions compared to traditional metrics, which demonstrated negative correlations due to sensitivity to caption length.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EmoSURA framework provides a more reliable evaluation method for emotional speech captions by addressing fundamental limitations of existing metrics through atomic verification and audio-grounded validation.

title: A Survey on Cloud-Based 6G Deployments: Current Solutions, Future Directions and Open Challenges
summary: The next generation of cellular networks is designed to provide ubiquitous connectivity to a wide range of devices. As Telecommunication Service Providers (TSPs) increasingly collaborate with public cloud providers to deploy 5G and beyond networks, a fundamental shift is underway, from hardware-bound Physical Network Functions (PNFs) to cloud-native, containerized deployments managed through platforms like Kubernetes. While this transition promises greater scalability, flexibility, and cost efficiency, it also introduces a complex set of technical and operational challenges that must be thoroughly understood before large-scale cellular deployments can take place in cloud environments. In this survey, we present a structured taxonomy that categorizes the design space of cloud-based cellular deployments across four dimensions: deployment architecture, resource management and orchestration, multi-tenancy and isolation, and economic and ownership models. Using this taxonomy as a foundation, we critically analyze six key investigation areas, security and privacy, scalability and elasticity, performance and latency, cost optimization, resilience and fault management, and compliance and sovereignty, examining each through a cloud-native lens. To benchmark the state of industry adoption, we examine the deployment strategies of leading Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) providers, namely Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Finally, we identify emerging trends such as AI-driven orchestration, quantum-safe protocols for virtualized network functions, and serverless networking for 6G, while articulating the open challenges that remain in realizing robust, scalable cloud-based cellular networks.

AI.tldr: Survey paper analyzing cloud-native cellular network deployments, presenting a 4D taxonomy and examining six key investigation areas through industry case studies and emerging trends.
AI.motivation: The transition from hardware-bound Physical Network Functions (PNFs) to cloud-native, containerized deployments in 5G/beyond networks introduces complex technical and operational challenges that must be understood before large-scale cloud deployments can occur.
AI.method: Presents a structured taxonomy across four dimensions (deployment architecture, resource management/orchestration, multi-tenancy/isolation, economic/ownership models), critically analyzes six key investigation areas, examines industry adoption through IaaS provider case studies (AWS, Azure, GCP), and identifies emerging trends.
AI.result: Provides comprehensive analysis framework for cloud-based cellular deployments, benchmarks industry adoption strategies, and identifies emerging trends including AI-driven orchestration, quantum-safe protocols, and serverless networking for 6G.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a foundational taxonomy and analysis framework for understanding cloud-native cellular networks, identifies key challenges and industry practices, and outlines future research directions while highlighting open challenges for robust, scalable cloud-based cellular deployments.

title: SCENEBench: An Audio Understanding Benchmark Grounded in Assistive and Industrial Use Cases
comment: Accepted to EACL 2026 (Main Conference). 10 pages, 10 figures. Camera-ready version
summary: Advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled significant capabilities in audio processing, resulting in state-of-the-art models now known as Large Audio Language Models (LALMs). However, minimal work has been done to measure audio understanding beyond automatic speech recognition (ASR). This paper closes that gap by proposing a benchmark suite, SCENEBench (Spatial, Cross-lingual, Environmental, Non-speech Evaluation), that targets a broad form of audio comprehension across four real-world categories: background sound understanding, noise localization, cross-linguistic speech understanding, and vocal characterizer recognition. These four categories are selected based on understudied needs from accessibility technology and industrial noise monitoring. In addition to performance, we also measure model latency. The purpose of this benchmark suite is to assess audio beyond just what words are said - rather, how they are said and the non-speech components of the audio. Because our audio samples are synthetically constructed (e.g., by overlaying two natural audio samples), we further validate our benchmark against 20 natural audio items per task, sub-sampled from existing datasets to match our task criteria, to assess ecological validity. We assess five state-of-the-art LALMs and find critical gaps: performance varies across tasks, with some tasks performing below random chance and others achieving high accuracy. These results provide direction for targeted improvements in model capabilities.

AI.tldr: SCENEBench is a new benchmark suite for Large Audio Language Models that evaluates audio understanding beyond speech recognition across four categories: background sound understanding, noise localization, cross-lingual speech understanding, and vocal characterizer recognition.
AI.motivation: Current Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) focus primarily on automatic speech recognition, but lack comprehensive evaluation of broader audio understanding capabilities needed for real-world applications like accessibility technology and industrial noise monitoring.
AI.method: The authors propose SCENEBench, a benchmark suite with four evaluation categories: 1) background sound understanding, 2) noise localization, 3) cross-linguistic speech understanding, and 4) vocal characterizer recognition. They use synthetically constructed audio samples (overlaying natural audio) and validate with 20 natural audio items per task from existing datasets to ensure ecological validity. They also measure model latency alongside performance.
AI.result: Evaluation of five state-of-the-art LALMs reveals critical gaps: performance varies significantly across tasks, with some tasks performing below random chance while others achieve high accuracy. The benchmark successfully identifies specific areas where models need improvement.
AI.conclusion: SCENEBench provides a comprehensive framework for assessing audio understanding beyond speech recognition, revealing significant limitations in current LALMs and offering direction for targeted improvements in model capabilities for real-world applications.

title: Dynamic Average Consensus with Privacy Guarantees and Its Application to Battery Energy Storage Systems
summary: A privacy-preserving dynamic average consensus (DAC) algorithm is proposed that achieves consensus while preventing external eavesdroppers from inferring the reference signals and their derivatives. During the initialization phase, each agent generates a set of sinusoidal signals with randomly selected frequencies and exchanges them with its neighboring agents to construct a masking signal. Each agent masks its reference signals using this composite masking signal before executing the DAC update rule. It is shown that the developed scheme preserves the convergence properties of the conventional DAC framework while preventing information leakage to external eavesdroppers. Furthermore, the developed algorithm is applied to state-of-charge (SoC) balancing in a networked battery energy storage system to demonstrate its practical applicability. Simulation results validate the theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: A privacy-preserving DAC algorithm that masks reference signals with composite sinusoidal signals to prevent eavesdroppers from inferring sensitive information while maintaining convergence properties.
AI.motivation: To address privacy concerns in dynamic average consensus algorithms where external eavesdroppers could potentially infer sensitive reference signals and their derivatives, which is particularly critical in applications like battery energy storage systems.
AI.method: Each agent generates sinusoidal signals with random frequencies, exchanges them with neighbors to construct a composite masking signal, then masks reference signals before executing DAC update rules.
AI.result: The developed scheme preserves convergence properties of conventional DAC while preventing information leakage to external eavesdroppers, validated through simulation results.
AI.conclusion: The privacy-preserving DAC algorithm successfully protects sensitive information from eavesdroppers while maintaining system functionality, with practical applicability demonstrated in SoC balancing for battery energy storage systems.

title: AI-Enabled Data-driven Intelligence for Spectrum Demand Estimation
comment: Presented at an IEEE ICC 2025 Workshop and published in the conference proceedings
summary: Accurately forecasting spectrum demand is a key component for efficient spectrum resource allocation and management. With the rapid growth in demand for wireless services, mobile network operators and regulators face increasing challenges in ensuring adequate spectrum availability. This paper presents a data-driven approach leveraging artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) to estimate and manage spectrum demand. The approach uses multiple proxies of spectrum demand, drawing from site license data and derived from crowdsourced data. These proxies are validated against real-world mobile network traffic data to ensure reliability, achieving an R$^2$ value of 0.89 for an enhanced proxy. The proposed ML models are tested and validated across five major Canadian cities, demonstrating their generalizability and robustness. These contributions assist spectrum regulators in dynamic spectrum planning, enabling better resource allocation and policy adjustments to meet future network demands.

AI.tldr: AI/ML-based approach using multiple proxies (site license and crowdsourced data) to forecast spectrum demand, validated against real traffic data with R²=0.89, tested across five Canadian cities for generalizability.
AI.motivation: Accurate spectrum demand forecasting is crucial for efficient spectrum resource allocation and management, especially with rapid growth in wireless services demand, posing challenges for mobile network operators and regulators in ensuring adequate spectrum availability.
AI.method: Data-driven approach leveraging AI/ML with multiple proxies of spectrum demand: site license data and crowdsourced data, validated against real-world mobile network traffic data to ensure reliability.
AI.result: Achieved R² value of 0.89 for enhanced proxy validation; ML models tested and validated across five major Canadian cities, demonstrating generalizability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The approach assists spectrum regulators in dynamic spectrum planning, enabling better resource allocation and policy adjustments to meet future network demands.

title: Emergency Locator Transmitters in the Era of More Electric Aircraft: A Comprehensive Review of Energy, Integration and Safety Challenges
summary: The progressive electrification of aircraft systems under the more electric aircraft (MEA) paradigm is reshaping the design and qualification constraints of safety-critical avionics. Emergency locator transmitters (ELTs), which are essential for post-accident localization and search and rescue (SAR) operations, have evolved from legacy 121.5/243 MHz beacons to digitally encoded 406 MHz systems, typically retaining 121.5 MHz as a homing signal in combined units. In parallel, the modernization of the Cospas-Sarsat infrastructure, especially MEOSAR, together with multi-constellation global navigation satellite system (GNSS) integration and second-generation beacon capabilities, is reducing detection latency and enabling richer distress messaging. However, MEA platforms impose stricter constraints on available power, thermal management, wiring density, and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC). As a result, ELT performance increasingly depends not only on the device itself, but also on its installation conditions and on the aircraft's overall electrical environment. This review summarizes the ELT architectures and activation/operational cycles, outlines key technological milestones, and consolidates the main integration challenges for MEA, with emphasis on energy autonomy, battery qualification frameworks, EMC and installation practices, and survivability-driven failure modes (e.g., antenna/feedline damage, mounting, and post-impact shielding). Finally, emerging trends include ELT for distress tracking (DT), energy-based designs, advanced health monitoring, and certification-ready pathways for next-generation SAR services are discussed, highlighting research directions that can deliver demonstrable, certifiable gains in reliability, energy efficiency, and robust integration for future electrified aircraft.

AI.tldr: This review paper examines the integration challenges of Emergency Locator Transmitters (ELTs) in More Electric Aircraft (MEA), covering technological evolution, MEOSAR modernization, and key integration issues including power constraints, EMC, battery qualification, and survivability, while discussing emerging trends like distress tracking and advanced health monitoring.
AI.motivation: The progressive electrification of aircraft systems under the MEA paradigm imposes stricter constraints on safety-critical avionics like ELTs, creating integration challenges that affect performance, reliability, and certification pathways for next-generation search and rescue services.
AI.method: The paper conducts a comprehensive review that: 1) summarizes ELT architectures and activation/operational cycles, 2) outlines key technological milestones in ELT evolution and MEOSAR modernization, 3) consolidates main integration challenges for MEA platforms, and 4) discusses emerging trends and research directions.
AI.result: The review identifies that ELT performance in MEA platforms increasingly depends on installation conditions and aircraft electrical environment, not just the device itself. Key challenges include energy autonomy, battery qualification frameworks, EMC compliance, installation practices, and survivability-driven failure modes. Emerging trends point toward ELT for distress tracking, energy-based designs, advanced health monitoring, and certification-ready pathways.
AI.conclusion: Future research should focus on delivering demonstrable, certifiable gains in reliability, energy efficiency, and robust integration of ELTs for electrified aircraft, addressing the specific constraints imposed by MEA platforms while leveraging technological advancements in MEOSAR, GNSS integration, and second-generation beacon capabilities.

title: Towards Flexible Spectrum Access: Data-Driven Insights into Spectrum Demand
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Presented at IEEE VTC 2024, Washington, DC. Published in the IEEE conference proceedings
summary: In the diverse landscape of 6G networks, where wireless connectivity demands surge and spectrum resources remain limited, flexible spectrum access becomes paramount. The success of crafting such schemes hinges on our ability to accurately characterize spectrum demand patterns across space and time. This paper presents a data-driven methodology for estimating spectrum demand variations over space and identifying key drivers of these variations in the mobile broadband landscape. By leveraging geospatial analytics and machine learning, the methodology is applied to a case study in Canada to estimate spectrum demand dynamics in urban regions. Our proposed model captures 70\% of the variability in spectrum demand when trained on one urban area and tested on another. These insights empower regulators to navigate the complexities of 6G networks and devise effective policies to meet future network demands.

AI.tldr: Data-driven methodology using geospatial analytics and ML to estimate spectrum demand variations in 6G networks, achieving 70% accuracy when trained on one urban area and tested on another.
AI.motivation: In 6G networks with surging connectivity demands and limited spectrum resources, flexible spectrum access requires accurate characterization of spectrum demand patterns across space and time to enable effective policy-making.
AI.method: Data-driven methodology combining geospatial analytics and machine learning to estimate spectrum demand variations over space and identify key drivers of these variations in mobile broadband landscape.
AI.result: Proposed model captures 70% of variability in spectrum demand when trained on one urban area and tested on another urban region in Canada case study.
AI.conclusion: The methodology provides valuable insights for regulators to navigate 6G network complexities and devise effective policies to meet future network demands through better understanding of spectrum demand dynamics.
